topics.title: Present Simple vs Present Continuous
formBox: Present Simple: I work / I don't work / Do I work?
Present Continuous: I am working / I am not working / Am I working?

useNotes.label: Present Simple
points: Habits and routines: She gets up at 7 o'clock every morning.
Permanent facts and general truths: Water boils at 100°C.
Stative verbs (feelings, opinions, senses): I love jazz. / This soup tastes delicious.

useNotes.label: Present Continuous
points: Actions happening right now: She is reading a book at the moment.
Temporary situations: He is working from home this week.
Future arrangements: We are meeting the client on Friday.

commonErrors.note: AGREE is a stative verb — it has no continuous form.
HAVE meaning 'possess' is stative; only dynamic HAVE (eat/experience) takes -ing.
General scientific facts use the present simple, not the continuous.

questionGroups.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct form (present simple or present continuous) of the verb in brackets.

questions.prompt: She usually ___ (work) from home on Fridays.
I ___ (not / understand) this exercise. Can you help me?
We ___ (meet) Sarah for lunch tomorrow at 1pm.

topics.title: Past Simple vs Past Continuous
formBox: Past Simple: I worked / I didn't work / Did I work?
Past Continuous: I was working / I wasn't working / Was I working?

useNotes.label: Past Simple
points: Completed actions at a specific past time: She left the office at 6pm yesterday.
A sequence of completed actions: He entered the room, sat down, and opened his laptop.

useNotes.label: Past Continuous
points: An action in progress at a specific past moment: At 6pm yesterday, she was still working.
A background action interrupted by a shorter completed action: I was cooking dinner when the phone rang.

commonErrors.wrong: When I arrived, she was leaving already.
commonErrors.right: When I arrived, she had already left.
commonErrors.note: For an action completed BEFORE another past action, use the past perfect, not the past continuous.

commonErrors.wrong: I was living in Paris for two years, from 2015 to 2017.
commonErrors.right: I lived in Paris for two years, from 2015 to 2017.
commonErrors.note: A completed period with a clear start and end uses the past simple, not the continuous.

questionGroups.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct form (past simple or past continuous) of the verb in brackets.

questions.prompt: While I ___ (walk) to work yesterday, I saw an old friend.
They ___ (watch) TV when the power suddenly went out.
He ___ (call) me three times yesterday evening.
I ___ (try) to call you at 9pm, but you didn't answer.

topics.title: Modal Verbs of Obligation
formBox: Obligation: You must / have to finish this today.
No obligation: You don't have to come if you're busy.
Advice: You should see a doctor.

useNotes.label: MUST
points: Strong obligation, often from the speaker's own authority or feeling: I must remember to call my mother.
Formal rules and regulations: Passengers must fasten their seatbelts.

useNotes.label: HAVE TO
points: Obligation imposed by an external rule or circumstance: I have to wear a uniform at work.
More common than MUST in everyday spoken English for external rules.

useNotes.label: MUSTN'T vs DON'T HAVE TO
points: MUSTN'T = it is prohibited, you must not do it: You mustn't park here.
DON'T HAVE TO = there is no obligation, but you can if you want: You don't have to attend, it's optional.

useNotes.label: SHOULD
points: Advice or a recommendation, not an obligation: You should get more sleep.

commonErrors.note: MUST has no 'don't must' form — use MUSTN'T for prohibition.

commonErrors.wrong: Students must to bring a pen.
commonErrors.note: Modal verbs are followed by the base form, without 'to'.

commonErrors.wrong: You mustn't come if you're busy — it's optional.
commonErrors.right: You don't have to come if you're busy — it's optional.
commonErrors.note: MUSTN'T means prohibited, not optional. For 'no obligation', use DON'T HAVE TO.

questionGroups.instructions: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C, or D.

questions.statement: Sign in a hospital: “All visitors ___ report to reception.”
It's just a suggestion — you ___ come if you don't want to.
You ___ smoke inside the building — it's strictly forbidden.
I think you ___ apologise to her — it would be the right thing to do.
Students ___ bring their own pen — the school provides one for everyone.

topics.title: Articles: A / An / The
formBox: A / AN: used with singular countable nouns, first mention, non-specific.
THE: used when both speaker and listener know which specific thing is meant.
(no article): plural or uncountable nouns in general statements.

useNotes.label: A / AN
points: First mention of something: I saw a dog in the park.
One of many, non-specific: She's a teacher.
AN before a vowel SOUND, not just a vowel letter: an hour, an MBA, an honest person.

useNotes.label: THE
points: Second mention / already specific: I saw a dog. The dog was brown.
Unique things: the sun, the internet, the government.
Superlatives: the best, the tallest.

useNotes.label: No article
points: General or plural/uncountable statements: Dogs make loyal pets. / Water is essential for life.

commonErrors.note: AN is used before a vowel SOUND, not just a vowel letter — 'honest' starts with a silent H.

commonErrors.wrong: I love the music in general.
commonErrors.note: General, uncountable statements take no article, not THE.

commonErrors.wrong: He is the best student in a class.
commonErrors.right: He is the best student in the class.
commonErrors.note: A specific, already-identified class needs THE, not A.

questionGroups.instructions: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C, or D. D means no article is needed.

questions.statement: I saw ___ elephant at the zoo yesterday.
She works as ___ engineer at a tech company.
He is ___ most talented musician in the band.
___ Honesty is the best policy, they say.
